name: Minchiate — The 97-Card Florentine Tarot
description:
# Minchiate

## At a glance

A Florentine super-sized tarot game: **97 cards** with **40 trumps** instead of 22 —
adding the zodiac, the four elements, and the remaining theological/cardinal virtues.
Despite all that cosmic imagery it is a **game**, hugely popular for three centuries,
and a flagship of the **A-order** branch.

## Origin & dating

First recorded as **germini** in **1506** (named for the Gemini card, highest of the new
trumps). The expanded 97-card pack so outsold the older form that the older name
*minchiate* migrated to the big deck in the 1600s. From Florence it spread across Italy
and into France through the 17th century.

## Provenance & evidence

Many printed editions survive (e.g. the well-known 18th–19th-century Florentine
*Minchiate Fiorentine*). Rules are preserved in gaming literature; pagat.com documents
the game's structure. The expansion is the headline evidence: trumps nearly doubled to
absorb astrological and elemental figures.

## Game or divination?

**Game.** This is the deck that most tempts an esoteric misreading — zodiac! elements!
virtues! — yet every record treats those cards as **trump ranks in a trick-taking
game**, not oracle cards. The cosmology is decorative/encyclopedic, the same Renaissance
visual vocabulary as the Mantegna prints, not a divinatory key.

## The fear question

**No fear.** A mainstream parlor and gambling game for centuries. The familiar caveat
applies — moralists disliked *gambling* — but Minchiate as such was respectable
entertainment, openly printed and sold.

## Counter-voices

Because Minchiate looks like a "complete" astrological tarot, occult writers sometimes
present it as a survival of the *true*, larger sacred system that the 22-trump decks
supposedly censored. The evidence runs the other way: the 22-trump form is older and the
40-trump Minchiate is an **expansion for richer play**, not a recovered esoteric
original.

## Open questions / corrections owed

- Minchiate's zodiac/element trumps have **no standard equivalent** in 22-trump tarot —
  the grammar should resist forcing a one-to-one mapping (the repo already flags this).
- Relationship between *minchiate* (77-card) and *germini* (97-card) naming needs a clean
  timeline in the grammar.

## Sources & further reading

- "Minchiate," Wikipedia; "Minchiate," pagat.com (rules).
- "Minchiate Fiorentine," World of Playing Cards.

---
*Research notes — AI-assisted first-pass draft, pending review by the maintainer and the Tarot History Forum. Skeptical, document-first (Dummett spine). Corrections welcome via pending edit.*

sections.Iconography: A ragged madman wanders, sometimes with feathers and a hobby-horse. Unnumbered, he is the wild card outside the count.
sections.Meaning: Folly and freedom, the spirit outside the order; in play the Fool excuses one from following suit.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: A seated, crowned papal figure. The first of five near-identical 'Papi'.
sections.Meaning: Minchiate dissolves the Popess, Empress, Emperor, Pope, and Love of ordinary tarot into five ranked 'Papi' (Popes) — the powers of Church and Empire flattened into one interchangeable office.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: A second crowned Papa, traditionally identified with the Empress.
sections.Meaning: The second of the five Papi, carrying the old Empress. The five rank only against each other in the game.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: A third Papa, identified with the Emperor.
sections.Meaning: The Emperor folded into the Papi sequence — temporal power made one of five equals.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: A fourth Papa, the Pope proper.
sections.Meaning: Spiritual authority, the Pope, ranked fourth among the five Papi.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: A fifth Papa, here carrying the old card of Love — sometimes a couple beneath Cupid.
sections.Meaning: Love absorbed into the Papi group; the highest of the five.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: A woman pours liquid between two vessels.
sections.Meaning: Temperance — the first of the cardinal virtues. Minchiate is the deck that carries the COMPLETE set of seven virtues.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: A woman subdues a lion or breaks a column.

sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: An enthroned woman with sword and scales.
sections.Meaning: Justice — the cardinal virtue of measure and right judgment.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: A wheel turned by Fortune, figures rising and falling.

sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. sections.Iconography: A triumphal car bearing a crowned victor.
sections.Meaning: Triumph and advance — the victory parade that gives the trumps their name.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. sections.Iconography: A bent old man with a stick, sometimes an hourglass — 'Il Gobbo', the Hunchback, the Minchiate name for Time / the Hermit.
sections.Meaning: Time, age, and solitude; the bent figure measuring his hours.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: A youth hung by one foot from a beam.
sections.Meaning: Suspension, reversal, the traitor's shame; the world seen upside-down.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. sections.Iconography: A tower struck and burning, figures falling — 'the House of the Devil', the Minchiate Tower.
sections.Meaning: Sudden ruin, the breaking of false structures, catastrophe as release.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: A woman with an anchor gazes upward in prayer.
sections.Meaning: Hope — a theological virtue. With Faith and Charity it gives Minchiate the three theological virtues ordinary tarot lacks.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: A woman with a mirror and serpent, contemplating herself.
sections.Meaning: Prudence — the fourth cardinal virtue, missing from standard tarot and restored here, completing the cardinal set.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. sections.Iconography: A woman nursing children, the image of Caritas.
sections.Meaning: Charity — the theological virtue of self-giving love, named the greatest of the three.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. sections.Iconography: A salamander in flames, or a burning scene — the element Fire.
sections.Meaning: Fire — will, transformation, the active spark. The first of the four classical elements unique to Minchiate among early decks.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. sections.Iconography: Ace of Coins: the suit-emblems of coins arranged in the Florentine pattern, with the elaborate borders typical of the Minchiate.
sections.Meaning: Ace of Coins — the root and seed of the suit, within money, body, and the material world.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.About these Cards: Where ordinary tarot has the Popess, Empress, Emperor, Pope, and Love, the Minchiate has five ranked but near-identical 'Papi' (Popes). The Popess — the heretical card of the Visconti decks — is gone entirely. Church and Empire are flattened into one interchangeable office.
sections.Why this Matters: The disappearance of the Popess and the merging of the powers shows the tarot's imagery being sanitised and standardised as the deck spread through Counter-Reformation Italy.

sections.About these Cards: The Minchiate is the deck of the COMPLETE seven virtues. To the three cardinal virtues of ordinary tarot (Temperance, Fortitude, Justice) it restores the fourth — Prudence — and adds the three theological virtues, Faith, Hope, and Charity.
sections.Why this Matters: Only the Cary-Yale (with its three theological virtues) and the Minchiate (with all seven) treat the tarot as a complete moral cosmos. The Minchiate makes the virtue programme total.

sections.About these Cards: Fire, Water, Earth, and Air enter the trump sequence as cards in their own right — a feature found in no standard tarot. With the twelve zodiac signs above them, the Minchiate builds a whole cosmology into the deck.
sections.How to Use: Read the elements as the building blocks beneath the zodiac: the four qualities from which the twelve signs are composed.

items.name: The Twelve Signs of the Zodiac

sections.About these Cards: The twelve zodiac signs form the longest stretch of the Minchiate trumps — a full astrological wheel set into the deck (in Minchiate's own idiosyncratic order, beginning with Libra). No other historical tarot contains them.
sections.How to Use: Read each as its sign — element, mode, and ruling planet — turning the deck into a portable zodiac.

items.name: The Arie — The Five Supreme Cards

sections.About these Cards: The five highest cards — Star, Moon, Sun, World, and the Trumpets (Le Trombe, the Last Judgement) — are the 'Arie', unnumbered and supreme in play, crowning the whole 97-card edifice.
sections.How to Use: These are the deck's heavens: the celestial lights and the final trumpet above the zodiac, the elements, and all the world below.

sections.About this Suit: The Batons (Bastoni) of the Minchiate, suit of Fire. Each suit keeps the full court of four — and in Cups and Coins the page is a woman, while the knights ride as centaurs.
sections.How to Use: Read the suit for will in the everyday world beneath the great cosmological trumps.

sections.About this Suit: The Coins (Denari) of the Minchiate, suit of Earth. Each suit keeps the full court of four — and in Cups and Coins the page is a woman, while the knights ride as centaurs.
sections.How to Use: Read the suit for money in the everyday world beneath the great cosmological trumps.

sections.About this Suit: The Cups (Coppe) of the Minchiate, suit of Water. Each suit keeps the full court of four — and in Cups and Coins the page is a woman, while the knights ride as centaurs.
sections.How to Use: Read the suit for feeling in the everyday world beneath the great cosmological trumps.

sections.About this Suit: The Swords (Spade) of the Minchiate, suit of Air. Each suit keeps the full court of four — and in Cups and Coins the page is a woman, while the knights ride as centaurs.
sections.How to Use: Read the suit for mind in the everyday world beneath the great cosmological trumps.
